nativeRange.habitat: Has been recorded in tropical lagoons, bays, and reef environments. Generally associates with rubble, rockwork, and crevices on shallow tropical reefs where echinoderm prey is plentiful.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices and overhangs
shaded hiding spots for the daytime
mature reef tank with stable parameters

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on the tube feet of echinoderms (sea stars, sea urchins, brittle stars, sand dollars). In captivity, generally accepts small portions of frozen meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, cockle, and finely-chopped fish; daily small feedings are typically advised given the shrimp's tiny size.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (firefish, small gobies)
snails and other peaceful clean-up-crew invertebrates
conspecifics — best kept as a pair in a nano reef
avoidWith: sea stars, sea urchins, brittle stars, sand dollars (will predate their tube feet)
larger predatory fish (wrasses, hawkfish, triggerfish, large angelfish)
aggressive shrimp (Coral Banded Shrimp, Arrow Crab)

summary: A tiny (~1.5-2.5 cm) yellow-and-black banded shrimp that has been documented feeding on the tube feet of echinoderms. Generally considered reef safe to corals but predatory toward sea stars and urchins.
careNotes: Gnathophyllum americanum is a small, brightly-coloured shrimp commonly sold as the 'Bumblebee Shrimp' or 'Striped Harlequin Shrimp'. It is best kept singly or as a pair in a small, mature nano reef where it can be observed easily — adults rarely exceed 2.5 cm. Its echinoderm-tube-feet diet specialism is the source of most husbandry friction: it will hunt and damage ornamental sea stars, urchins, brittle stars, and sand dollars, so tanks housing those animals are not suitable. Some keepers buy these shrimp specifically to control nuisance Asterina starfish, but be aware that Asterina lack the conventional tube-feet morphology and bumblebee shrimp are not a reliable control for them. The species is acutely sensitive to copper-based medications and to nitrate buildup; iodine and trace-element supplementation are commonly advised to support healthy molting. Because of their small size and pale shells just after molting, they are vulnerable to predation by larger reef fish — a peaceful, fish-light nano setup is generally the safest home. Source values agreed closely: temperature 22-26°C, pH 8.0-8.4, specific gravity 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12. Care-level ratings spanned 'intermediate' (BlueZoo Aquatics) to 'difficult' (LiveAquaria); advanced is used here as the middle value, reflecting the specialised diet and copper sensitivity. Note that the trade common name 'Bumblebee Shrimp' also refers to a freshwater Caridina species (Caridina sp. 'Bumblebee') — the two are unrelated and require very different husbandry.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered impractical and is not commonly reported in the hobby. Like many marine caridean shrimp, larvae are believed to pass through an extended planktonic stage that is extremely difficult to rear under aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water-parameter ranges (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12), diet specifics (echinoderm tube feet plus accepted prepared foods), reef-safe status, and copper-sensitivity warning.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (1 in / 2.5 cm), tank minimum (10 US gal ~ 40 L), peaceful temperament, carnivore diet, and reef-safe status. Listed care level as 'Intermediate' (one ordinal step below LiveAquaria's 'Difficult'); advanced was selected as the middle value.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Bumble Bee Shrimp Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Third source for cross-verification. Confirmed temperature 22-26°C, pH 8.0-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, 10 US gal minimum, ~2.5 cm adult size, and lifespan of 2-3 years. Noted that Asterina starfish lack the tube-feet morphology this shrimp targets, so it is not a reliable Asterina-control option despite the popular myth.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Gnathophyllum americanum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy placement (Palaemonidae / Decapoda / Caridea) and confirmation of tropical lagoon-bay-reef habitat. Adult length confirmed at up to ~25 mm.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval for this species. Iodine and trace-element supplementation are commonly advised to support healthy molting; old exoskeletons are typically left in the tank as they are often consumed for calcium reuptake. Newly-molted shrimp are very vulnerable and should have a secure hiding spot.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered impractical. Larvae are believed to pass through an extended planktonic phase that is extremely difficult to rear in aquarium conditions, and no widely-reported captive-breeding protocol exists.
